subject: [PATCH v3 1/3] iova: convert from rbtree to maple tree

2026-06-03.patch_summary: This patch replaces the hand-rolled red-black tree in the IOVA allocator with a Maple tree, a B-tree variant designed for range tracking and gap searching. The change reduces memory usage per IOVA entry from approximately 64 bytes to around 44-57 bytes, while also improving search performance by reducing the search depth to O(log_10 n). Additionally, the patch simplifies the code by eliminating the need for retry loops and successor gap fixups.

reviews.author: Liam Howlett
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Liam Howlett questioned the use of an external lock when the spinlock is intended to protect the tree, suggesting a potential inconsistency in locking behavior.

Reviewer Liam Howlett noted that the alloc_fail label in the error recovery path may overwrite the max32_alloc_size, which is intended to ensure 32-bit safety, and requested that this be fixed.

Reviewer Liam Howlett questioned the possibility of a specific alignment scenario occurring in the IOVA allocator, specifically that the worst-case alignment gap found by mas_empty_area_rev() could be smaller than both the start and end of the search range.

Reviewer noted that the maple state can be used to avoid rewalking the tree when searching for specific slots, potentially reducing dereference overhead.
reviews.raw_body:
If the spinlock is only used to protect the tree, why are you using an
external lock?

---

This check seems to ensure the request is either both 32bit safe or it's
fine to pass.  If a 64bit allocation uses a larger size and we fail
later, aren't we overwriting the 32bit size safety in your alloc_fail
label?  It seems wrong to be changing the max32_alloc_size in the error
recovery.

This also could happen outside the spinlock?  I'm not sure why it was
inside to begin with.

---

Neither of these can ever happen..?  You've searched for a gap that's
the worst case alignment and now you're ensuring the offset off the end
aligned isn't smaller than the start of the gap or the end of your
search.  I'm no LLM, I don't think this is possible.

---

The maple state keeps track of where things are, so if you have a maple
state pointing at the correct slot, then you can avoid rewalking the
tree.  This is rather complicated, but it will save you a hand full of
dereferences if you need them.

2026-06-17.patch_summary: This patch replaces the hand-rolled red-black tree in the IOVA allocator with a Maple tree, a B-tree variant designed for range tracking and gap searching. The change reduces memory usage per IOVA entry from around 64 bytes to around 44-57 bytes, while also improving search performance by reducing the search depth to O(log_10 n). The patch also simplifies the code by eliminating the need for retry loops and successor gap fixups.
